event: The stoning of the Sabbath breaker in the wilderness
symbols: stones raised overhead — the instrument of capital punishment under the Mosaic law
fallen victim — the consequences of breaking the covenant
angry crowd — communal enforcement of the divine commandment
wilderness setting — the period of Israel's testing and formation under the law
wooden fence — the boundaries of the camp and the law
description: A vivid, detailed illustration showing a man lying fallen on a pile of stones as a crowd of Israelite men in the wilderness hurl large rocks at him. The attackers are bare-chested or lightly clothed, muscular, with intense expressions of zeal or anger. Some raise heavy stones above their heads. The victim lies on the ground amid the stones, his body battered. The setting is outdoors with a wooden fence or palisade in the background and hills beyond. The scene is rendered in gouache with precise detail.
theological_description: This scene depicts the execution prescribed in Numbers 15:32-36, where a man found gathering wood on the Sabbath was brought before Moses, and the LORD commanded that 'the man must die. The whole assembly must stone him outside the camp.' The severity of this punishment underscores the absolute seriousness with which God regarded Sabbath observance as the sign of the covenant between Himself and Israel (Exodus 31:13-17). The Sabbath was not merely a day of rest but a weekly reenactment of creation's completion and a prophetic anticipation of eschatological rest (Hebrews 4:9). Breaking the Sabbath was tantamount to breaking the covenant itself. This passage stands in deliberate tension with Jesus's later teaching that 'the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath' (Mark 2:27), and His declaration that 'the Son of Man is Lord even of the Sabbath' (Mark 2:28). The harshness of the old dispensation reveals, by contrast, the mercy of the new — where Christ fulfills the law and bears its penalties on behalf of all transgressors (Galatians 3:13).

scripture_refs.reason: The man found gathering sticks on the Sabbath and stoned by the assembly — the direct event depicted
The Sabbath command with the death penalty for violation — the legal basis for the stoning
Jesus declaring the Sabbath made for man and Himself Lord of the Sabbath — the new covenant reinterpretation
Christ becoming a curse for us to redeem us from the curse of the law
